title: Monorepo Tooling: Three Scopes — Quick Check

questions.q: What are the three scopes managed by pnpm workspaces, Turborepo, and Docker Compose, and why are these scopes non-overlapping?
questions.a: pnpm workspaces manages the package registry scope: which packages exist, how they link to each other via workspace:*, and how their node_modules are deduplicated. Turborepo manages the task execution scope: which build/test/lint tasks exist across packages, their inter-package dependency order, and what outputs can be cached. Docker Compose manages the runtime service scope: which processes run (postgres, redis, server, client), their network topology, port mappings, health checks, and startup ordering. Non-overlapping: pnpm doesn't know about build caching; Turborepo doesn't know about runtime containers; Docker Compose doesn't know about package linking.

questions.q: What observable signal indicates Turborepo should be added to an existing pnpm monorepo?
questions.a: The signal is sequential build execution of packages that could build in parallel. Specifically: the CI or local build runs db first (correct, dependency), then builds client and server sequentially even though client has no dependency on server and could build in parallel with it after db completes. Turborepo's `dependsOn: ['^build']` pipeline configuration enables this parallelism with hash-based output caching. Adding Turborepo speculatively before this bottleneck appears is premature — it adds turbo.json configuration complexity with no measurable benefit.

questions.q: Explain the node_modules volume exclusion pattern in Docker Compose bind mounts: what it is, what it prevents, and what Docker mechanism implements it.
questions.a: The pattern: when a bind mount maps the host directory into the container (e.g. `- .:/app`), it also maps the host's node_modules directory. Host node_modules contain OS-specific compiled binaries (e.g. native Node.js addons compiled for Windows or macOS). The container runs Linux — those binaries are incompatible. The exclusion `- /app/node_modules` adds a second volume mount at the node_modules path specifically, which Docker treats as an anonymous Docker-managed volume. Docker's volume mount priority means the anonymous volume (container-native node_modules) takes precedence over the bind mount at that subdirectory, preserving the container's correctly-compiled dependencies.

questions.q: What does a Docker Compose health check actually test, and why is `condition: service_healthy` necessary for dependent services?
questions.a: A health check runs a command inside the running container on a schedule (e.g. `pg_isready -U meridian`) and exits 0 if the service is ready, non-zero otherwise. Docker reports the container as 'healthy' only after consecutive successful health checks. `condition: service_healthy` in a dependent service's `depends_on` block means: don't start this container until the dependency's health check has passed. Without it, `depends_on` only waits for the container process to start (milliseconds), not for the service to be ready (PostgreSQL init can take 2–10 seconds). Server would start and immediately fail to connect to postgres without the health check gate.
